title: Rust performance investigation --status in_progress
description:
## Goal
Make Rust cold-load meaningfully faster than Python for record loading; target up to ~10x if feasible.

## Context
Rust version currently performs similar to Python, which is unexpected. We need to leverage Rust for speed (e.g., parallel load) and empirically validate improvements.

## Approach
For each performance idea: implement, ensure specs pass, benchmark vs Rust baseline and Python, and keep only if gains justify complexity. If not, revert to baseline and record what was tried/learned.

## Tracking
Create one story per idea with separate notes; work may use Git worktrees for isolation.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text: Context from user (2026-02-23): Surprised Rust perf matches Python. Expect up to ~10x faster for this task. Explore multiple Rust performance ideas (e.g., parallel loading), implement each, verify specs/tests, benchmark vs Rust baseline and Python. Keep/merge only if empirical gains justify added complexity; otherwise revert to baseline and note failed idea + learnings. Create a new story per idea and track notes separately. Git worktrees ok for isolation.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
## Conclusion (2026-02-23)
The benchmark harness is Python-based. When `VIRTUUS_BENCH_BACKEND=rust`, it tries to import `virtuus._rust`; if that import fails it silently falls back to the Python backend.

On this machine, the Rust PyO3 extension currently **does not build**, so `virtuus._rust` is not importable. As a result, attempts to run “Rust” benchmarks are actually exercising the Python backend. This explains why historical results show Rust and Python with equivalent performance.

Evidence:
- `maturin develop --release` fails with compile errors in `rust/src/python.rs` (PyO3 API/borrow issues) → `virtuus._rust` missing.
- Benchmark JSON does not record backend; Python harness defaults to Python when Rust import fails.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
## Progress (2026-02-23)
- Fixed PyO3 build issues in `rust/src/python.rs`; `maturin develop --release` now succeeds and `virtuus._rust` imports.
- Captured 10k yardstick baselines:
  - Python full_database_cold_load: 3354.440 ms
  - Rust full_database_cold_load: 2929.900 ms

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text: Done: CI green and semantic-release published 0.2.1; PyO3 build fixed and 10k baselines recorded. Next: close epic. Blockers: none.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text: Correction: reopened; this epic is still in progress. Next: continue Rust perf investigations. Blockers: none.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
## Rust-native benchmark update (2026-02-23)
Ran Rust directly via CLI (no Python harness). `cargo run --release --bin virtuus -- query` with schema loading all tables, 10k dataset. Mean load+query time: **2807.248 ms** (3 runs). Details logged on task virt-4a074a.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
## Experiment result: Parallel IO (Rayon)
Tried parallelizing `Table::load_from_dir` with Rayon (read+parse in parallel, sequential put). Benchmarks at 10k `full_database_cold_load` showed **no meaningful improvement** (mean ~2934 ms vs prior ~2930 ms). Changes were reverted and dependency removed.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
## Experiment result: byte reads + preallocation
Tried `fs::read` + `serde_json::from_slice` with pre-allocation in `Table::load_from_dir`. 10k `full_database_cold_load` showed no meaningful improvement (median ~2884 ms, mean worse due outlier). Reverted change.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
## Win: Load is now read-only
Changed `load_from_dir` to skip disk writes during load (Python + Rust), aligning with README (only `put`/`delete` write). 10k `full_database_cold_load` improved from ~2.9–3.3s to ~0.48s (mean Rust 478.758 ms, Python 492.872 ms). Keeping change.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text: New experiment: parallel JSON parsing in Rust load_from_dir (Rayon). Bench (10k full_database_cold_load): baseline mean 478.758 ms -> new mean 388.146 ms (~19% faster). CI passed; change committed in repo.
